figures.caption: Figure 1: Illustration of safety degradation. Given a harmful query, activation drift may cause the model to bypass refusal behavior and respond with unrelated procedural steps (e.g., making soup), while weight drift directly leads to unsafe outputs; our protected model constrains both activations and weights to preserve safety behavior.

Figure 5: Comparison of L2 norm distance distributions between fine-tuned model and safety baseline outputs/activations on harmful inputs for baseline methods and our CWAC, on Llama-2-7B (left) and Gemma-2-9B (right). Smaller L2 distance indicates better preservation of safety alignment.
Table 5: Ablation results comparing CWAC (weight and activation constraints) with its single-level variants (Weightonly and Activation-only) across four LLMs.

Table 1: Performance comparison on downstream tasks (SST-2, AGNEWS, GSM8K) and safety preservation across four widely adopted LLMs. ↑ indicates higher is better and ↓ indicates lower is better. Best results are shown in bold, and second-best results are underlined.
Table 2: Generalization results on additional downstream tasks, evaluating safety and utility on PubMedQA for medical question answering and AlpacaEval for instruction-following assessment.
Figure 4: Harmful scores across different fine-tuning epochs for CWAC ablations and baseline methods on four models.
Figure 2: Illustration of CWAC’s coupled constraints: activation-level regularization (top) and weight-level safety subspace construction (bottom).
Table 6: Qualitative comparison of responses to harmful prompts. The SFT model often generates harmful or misleading content, while our CWAC model consistently refuses to do so.
